concept.headline: Crystal has expressive static types with familiar literals.
concept.explanation: Crystal supports numeric, text, boolean, and nil values with strong compile-time checking. You can model optional values with union types like String?.
concept.syntax:
age = 30
price = 19.99
active = true
grade = 'A'
name = "Crystal"
optional_name : String? = nil
keyPoints: Nil combines with unions for optional data

demo.intro: See common Crystal types and their values in action.
demo.code:
age = 30
price = 19.99
active = true
grade = 'A'
name = "Crystal"
puts "#{age}, #{price}, #{active}, #{grade}, #{name}"

explanation.misconception: Integers and floats are not interchangeable without conversion
Only true/false are valid Bool values
Printing does not alter stored types

prediction.question: Which declaration creates a Char in Crystal?
prediction.explanation: Single quotes represent Char literals in Crystal.

explanation.afterDemo: Understanding data types helps you avoid subtle bugs and read compiler messages quickly.
keyTakeaways: Char uses single quotes, String uses double quotes.
Optional values are modeled with unions including Nil.

practice.prompt: Declare title as String, score as Int, pass as Bool, then print: "StepViz 100 true".

feedback.successMessage: Great. Your declarations and output are correct.
retryHints: Print all three values in one line

summary.title: Data Types — Complete
takeaways: Crystal types are static and expressive.
Literal style determines many inferred types.
Optional fields commonly use T? unions.
